The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ERT-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.1 Introduction to Ecosystems
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers introduction to ecosystems for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that an ecosystem is a community of living organisms (biotic factors) interacting with their nonliving environment (abiotic factors) through energy flows and nutrient cycling. Key abiotic factors include temperature, precipitation, sunlight, soil composition, and wind. Biotic factors include producers, consumers, decomposers, and their interactions such as predation, competition, mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism. Students must distinguish between biomes and ecosystems, and recognize that ecosystems exist at multiple scales from a small pond to the entire biosphere. Connect to biogeochemical cycles, energy flow, and biodiversity. AP exam note: questions frequently require students to identify abiotic and biotic factors in a given scenario and predict how changes to one factor affect others. Vocabulary: ecosystem, biotic, abiotic, biome, biosphere, community, population, habitat, niche, producer, consumer, decomposer.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ERT-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.2 Terrestrial Biomes
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers terrestrial biomes for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that terrestrial biomes are large geographic regions classified primarily by climate (temperature and precipitation), which determines the dominant vegetation and animal communities. Major biomes include tropical rainforest (high biodiversity, warm and wet year-round), savanna (grassland with scattered trees, seasonal rainfall), desert (very low precipitation, extreme temperatures), temperate grassland (moderate precipitation, rich soils), temperate deciduous forest (distinct seasons, broadleaf trees), boreal forest/taiga (cold winters, coniferous trees), and tundra (permafrost, very short growing season). Students must use a climatograph to identify biomes and predict species composition. Connect to climate patterns, global wind patterns, latitude, and altitude effects. AP exam note: interpreting climatographs to identify biomes is a frequently tested skill. Vocabulary: biome, climatograph, tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, temperate grassland, temperate forest, taiga, tundra, permafrost, canopy.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ERT-1.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.3 Aquatic Biomes
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers aquatic biomes for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand that aquatic biomes are classified as freshwater or marine, and further divided by characteristics such as salinity, depth, temperature, and flow rate. Freshwater biomes include streams, rivers, lakes, ponds, and wetlands. Marine biomes include the open ocean, coral reefs, estuaries, and intertidal zones. Estuaries, where freshwater meets saltwater, are among the most productive ecosystems on Earth and serve as critical nursery habitats. Key factors affecting aquatic ecosystems include dissolved oxygen, temperature, salinity, turbidity, and nutrient levels. Students must compare and contrast different aquatic zones (photic vs. aphotic, benthic vs. pelagic, littoral vs. limnetic). Connect to nutrient cycling, eutrophication, and human impacts on water quality. AP exam note: understanding zonation in lakes and oceans is essential. Vocabulary: estuary, salinity, photic zone, aphotic zone, benthic, pelagic, littoral, limnetic, wetland, coral reef, intertidal.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.4 The Carbon Cycle
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers the carbon cycle for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that carbon cycles through the atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere via processes including photosynthesis (removes CO2 from atmosphere), cellular respiration (releases CO2), decomposition, combustion of fossil fuels, and ocean absorption. Major carbon reservoirs include the atmosphere (as CO2 and CH4), oceans (dissolved CO2 and carbonates), fossil fuels, sedimentary rocks, and living biomass. Human activities, especially burning fossil fuels and deforestation, have significantly increased atmospheric CO2, intensifying the greenhouse effect. Students must diagram carbon flow between reservoirs and identify how human activities alter the natural balance. Connect to climate change, ocean acidification, and the greenhouse effect. AP exam note: free-response questions commonly ask students to trace carbon through the cycle and explain anthropogenic disruptions. Vocabulary: carbon cycle, photosynthesis, respiration, combustion, decomposition, fossil fuels, carbon sink, carbon source, reservoir, flux.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.5) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.5 The Nitrogen Cycle
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers the nitrogen cycle for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand that nitrogen (N2) makes up 78% of the atmosphere but is unavailable to most organisms in its gaseous form. Key processes include nitrogen fixation (conversion of N2 to ammonia by bacteria or lightning), nitrification (conversion of ammonia to nitrites and nitrates by bacteria), assimilation (uptake by plants), ammonification (decomposition releasing ammonia), and denitrification (conversion of nitrates back to N2). Nitrogen-fixing bacteria live freely in soil or in mutualistic associations with legume root nodules. Human activities disrupt the cycle through the Haber-Bosch process, excessive fertilizer use, and burning fossil fuels, leading to eutrophication and dead zones. Connect to agricultural practices, water pollution, and acid deposition. AP exam note: students must identify each step and the organisms involved. Vocabulary: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification, ammonification, assimilation, Haber-Bosch process, legume, nitrate, ammonia.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.6) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.6 The Phosphorus Cycle
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers the phosphorus cycle for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand that phosphorus does not have a significant atmospheric phase, distinguishing it from carbon and nitrogen cycles. Phosphorus cycles through rocks, soil, water, and living organisms. It enters the cycle through the weathering of phosphate-containing rocks and is absorbed by plants as inorganic phosphate (PO4^3-) from soil. Animals obtain phosphorus by consuming plants. Decomposition returns phosphorus to the soil. Over geological time, phosphorus is incorporated into sedimentary rock and uplifted through tectonic activity. Human activities, especially mining phosphate rock for fertilizers and detergent use, accelerate phosphorus input into aquatic systems, causing eutrophication. Phosphorus is often the limiting nutrient in freshwater ecosystems. Connect to eutrophication, algal blooms, and agricultural runoff. AP exam note: students must explain why phosphorus is the limiting nutrient and how excess input causes ecological damage. Vocabulary: phosphorus cycle, phosphate, weathering, limiting nutrient, sedimentation, eutrophication, fertilizer runoff.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.D (Unit 1, Topic 1.7) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.7 The Hydrologic (Water) Cycle
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers the hydrologic (water) cycle for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). Students should understand that water cycles continuously through evaporation, transpiration (collectively evapotranspiration), condensation, precipitation, infiltration, and surface runoff. Major water reservoirs include oceans (largest, ~97% of all water), glaciers and ice caps, groundwater, surface freshwater, and the atmosphere. Solar energy drives evaporation, and gravity drives precipitation, runoff, and infiltration. Groundwater is stored in aquifers and accessed through wells. Human activities alter the water cycle through deforestation (increased runoff, decreased transpiration), urbanization (increased impervious surfaces and runoff), irrigation (groundwater depletion), and dam construction. Connect to watersheds, flooding, drought, and water resource management. AP exam note: students must trace water through the cycle and analyze how land-use changes affect water distribution. Vocabulary: evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, runoff, aquifer, groundwater, watershed, impervious surface.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.E (Unit 1, Topic 1.8) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.8 Primary Productivity
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers primary productivity for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.E, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). Students should understand that primary productivity measures the rate at which producers (autotrophs) convert solar energy into chemical energy through photosynthesis. Gross primary productivity (GPP) is the total amount of energy fixed by producers. Net primary productivity (NPP) equals GPP minus the energy used by producers for cellular respiration (R): NPP = GPP - R. NPP represents the energy available to consumers in an ecosystem. Factors limiting primary productivity include sunlight, water, temperature, and nutrient availability. Tropical rainforests and estuaries have the highest NPP among terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems, respectively. Open ocean has low productivity per unit area but high total productivity due to its vast size. Connect to energy flow, trophic levels, and the 10% rule. AP exam note: calculation of NPP from GPP and respiration data is commonly tested. Vocabulary: gross primary productivity, net primary productivity, autotroph, photosynthesis, respiration, biomass, limiting factor.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.F (Unit 1, Topic 1.9) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.9 Trophic Levels
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers trophic levels for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.F, Unit 1, Topic 1.9). Students should understand that trophic levels represent the feeding positions in an ecosystem. Producers (autotrophs) occupy the first trophic level and form the base of all food chains. Primary consumers (herbivores) are at the second level, secondary consumers (carnivores that eat herbivores) at the third, and tertiary consumers at the fourth. Decomposers and detritivores break down dead organic matter from all trophic levels, recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem. Each successive trophic level contains less energy and typically supports fewer organisms. Ecological pyramids of energy, biomass, and numbers visually represent these relationships. Connect to energy flow, the 10% rule, food webs, and bioaccumulation. AP exam note: students must construct and interpret ecological pyramids and explain why there are rarely more than four or five trophic levels. Vocabulary: trophic level, producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer, tertiary consumer, decomposer, detritivore, herbivore, carnivore, omnivore.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.G (Unit 1, Topic 1.10) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.10 Energy Flow and the 10% Rule
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers energy flow and the 10% rule for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.G, Unit 1, Topic 1.10). Students should understand that energy flows through ecosystems in one direction: from the sun to producers to consumers. At each trophic level, approximately 10% of the energy is transferred to the next level; the remaining 90% is lost primarily as heat through cellular respiration, with smaller amounts lost through incomplete digestion and movement. This means that if producers fix 10,000 kcal of energy, only about 1,000 kcal is available to primary consumers, 100 kcal to secondary consumers, and 10 kcal to tertiary consumers. This inefficiency explains why ecosystems support fewer organisms at higher trophic levels and why food chains are typically short. Connect to trophic levels, ecological pyramids, carrying capacity, and the environmental impact of meat consumption. AP exam note: quantitative calculations using the 10% rule are frequently tested. Vocabulary: energy flow, 10% rule, trophic efficiency, metabolic heat, ecological pyramid of energy, biomass.

The Living World: Ecosystems.skill: ENG-1.H (Unit 1, Topic 1.11) The Living World: Ecosystems - 1.11 Food Chains and Food Webs
The Living World: Ecosystems.prompt: This topic covers food chains and food webs for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-1.H, Unit 1, Topic 1.11). Students should understand that a food chain is a linear sequence showing how energy and nutrients pass from one organism to another through feeding relationships. A food web is a more realistic model showing the complex interconnected feeding relationships in an ecosystem, where organisms may feed at multiple trophic levels. Keystone species play a disproportionately large role in maintaining ecosystem structure relative to their abundance. Removing or adding a species from a food web can trigger trophic cascades that affect multiple other species. Students must analyze food webs to predict how changes in one population affect others. Connect to trophic levels, energy flow, biodiversity, and ecosystem stability. AP exam note: questions often present food web diagrams and ask students to predict the effects of population changes or species removal on other organisms. Vocabulary: food chain, food web, keystone species, trophic cascade, apex predator, producer, consumer, interdependence, ecosystem stability.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.1 Introduction to Biodiversity
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers introduction to biodiversity for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that biodiversity refers to the variety of life on Earth and is measured at three levels: genetic diversity (variation in genes within a species), species diversity (number and relative abundance of species in a community), and ecosystem diversity (variety of ecosystems in a region). Species richness is the total number of species present, while species evenness describes how equally individuals are distributed among species. High biodiversity generally increases ecosystem resilience and productivity. Biodiversity hotspots are regions with exceptionally high species richness and endemism that are under significant threat. Tropical rainforests and coral reefs are the most biodiverse terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems, respectively. Connect to ecosystem services, natural selection, and conservation biology. AP exam note: students must distinguish between species richness and evenness and explain why biodiversity matters for ecosystem function. Vocabulary: biodiversity, species richness, species evenness, genetic diversity, ecosystem diversity, endemic species, biodiversity hotspot, resilience.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.2 Ecosystem Services
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers ecosystem services for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that ecosystem services are the benefits that ecosystems provide to humans, categorized into four types: provisioning services (food, fresh water, timber, fiber, medicinal resources), regulating services (climate regulation, water purification, flood control, pollination, disease regulation), cultural services (recreation, aesthetic enjoyment, spiritual value, ecotourism), and supporting services (nutrient cycling, soil formation, primary productivity, habitat provision). Biodiversity underpins all ecosystem services, and the loss of species can diminish ecosystem function. Economic valuation of ecosystem services helps justify conservation efforts, though many services are difficult to quantify in monetary terms. Connect to biodiversity, sustainability, and human impacts on ecosystems. AP exam note: students must categorize ecosystem services and argue for conservation based on their value to human society. Vocabulary: ecosystem services, provisioning, regulating, cultural, supporting services, pollination, water purification, ecotourism, valuation.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.3 Island Biogeography
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers island biogeography for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand the theory of island biogeography, developed by MacArthur and Wilson, which predicts species richness on islands based on two key factors: island size and distance from the mainland. Larger islands support more species due to greater habitat diversity, more resources, and lower extinction rates. Islands closer to the mainland have higher immigration rates and thus more species. Species richness reaches equilibrium when immigration rate equals extinction rate. This theory applies not only to oceanic islands but also to habitat islands such as national parks, mountaintops, and forest fragments surrounded by developed land. Connect to habitat fragmentation, wildlife corridors, conservation planning, and biodiversity. AP exam note: students must predict relative species richness based on island size and distance and apply the theory to habitat fragments. Vocabulary: island biogeography, immigration rate, extinction rate, species-area relationship, equilibrium, habitat island, habitat fragmentation, wildlife corridor.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.D (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.4 Ecological Tolerance
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers ecological tolerance for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand that each species has a range of environmental conditions (such as temperature, pH, salinity, and moisture) within which it can survive, grow, and reproduce. The tolerance range includes a zone of physiological stress near the limits and an optimal range in the center where organisms perform best. Outside the tolerance range, organisms cannot survive. Species with broad tolerance ranges are often generalists, while those with narrow ranges are specialists. The distribution and abundance of species in ecosystems are strongly influenced by their tolerance ranges for critical abiotic factors. Indicator species have narrow tolerance ranges and can signal environmental quality. Connect to biomes, aquatic ecosystems, adaptations, and the effects of environmental change on species distributions. AP exam note: students must interpret tolerance curves and predict how environmental changes affect species survival. Vocabulary: tolerance range, optimal range, zone of stress, zone of intolerance, indicator species, limiting factor, abiotic factor.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.E (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.5 Natural Disruptions to Ecosystems
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers natural disruptions to ecosystems for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.E, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that ecosystems are dynamic and regularly experience natural disturbances that can alter community structure and biodiversity. Major natural disruptions include wildfires, volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, hurricanes, floods, droughts, tsunamis, and disease outbreaks. These disturbances can cause habitat destruction, species displacement, and changes in resource availability. However, many ecosystems are adapted to periodic disturbances. For example, some plant species require fire for seed germination (e.g., lodgepole pine), and periodic flooding deposits nutrient-rich sediment on floodplains. The intermediate disturbance hypothesis suggests that moderate levels of disturbance maintain the highest biodiversity by preventing competitive exclusion. Connect to ecological succession, resilience, adaptations, and human-caused disruptions. AP exam note: students must compare natural and human-caused disruptions and explain ecosystem recovery processes. Vocabulary: natural disturbance, wildfire, volcanic eruption, intermediate disturbance hypothesis, resilience, resistance, recovery, competitive exclusion.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.F (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.6 Adaptations
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers adaptations for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.F, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that adaptations are inherited traits that increase an organism's fitness (ability to survive and reproduce) in a particular environment. Adaptations arise through natural selection over many generations and can be structural (physical features like thick fur or camouflage), behavioral (migration, hibernation, nocturnal activity), or physiological (ability to tolerate extreme temperatures or produce toxins). Coevolution occurs when two or more species exert selective pressure on each other, such as in predator-prey relationships or pollinator-plant partnerships. Niche partitioning allows similar species to coexist by using different resources or occupying different microhabitats, reducing interspecific competition. Connect to natural selection, biodiversity, ecological tolerance, and species interactions. AP exam note: students must identify and explain specific adaptations in given scenarios and how they relate to environmental conditions. Vocabulary: adaptation, natural selection, fitness, structural adaptation, behavioral adaptation, physiological adaptation, coevolution, niche partitioning, mimicry, camouflage.

The Living World: Biodiversity.skill: ERT-2.G (Unit 2, Topic 2.7) The Living World: Biodiversity - 2.7 Ecological Succession
The Living World: Biodiversity.prompt: This topic covers ecological succession for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-2.G, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that ecological succession is the gradual process of change in species composition and community structure over time. Primary succession occurs in areas with no pre-existing soil, such as after a volcanic eruption or glacial retreat, beginning with pioneer species like lichens and mosses that break down rock and create soil. Secondary succession occurs in areas where soil remains after a disturbance, such as after a wildfire, flood, or abandoned farmland, and proceeds more rapidly than primary succession. Both types progress through a series of seral stages toward a climax community, which is a relatively stable and self-sustaining ecosystem. Species diversity generally increases during succession. Connect to biodiversity, natural disruptions, biomes, and human land use. AP exam note: students must distinguish between primary and secondary succession and describe the sequence of community changes. Vocabulary: ecological succession, primary succession, secondary succession, pioneer species, climax community, seral stage, lichen, colonization.

Populations.skill: ERT-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Populations - 3.1 Generalist and Specialist Species
Populations.prompt: This topic covers generalist and specialist species for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that species can be classified by the breadth of their ecological niche. Generalist species have broad niches, thriving in a wide variety of environmental conditions and using diverse food sources (e.g., raccoons, cockroaches, white-tailed deer). They tend to be more adaptable to environmental change. Specialist species have narrow niches, relying on specific habitats, food sources, or environmental conditions (e.g., koalas eating only eucalyptus, giant pandas eating bamboo). Specialists are more vulnerable to extinction when their specific resources are disrupted. The distinction between generalists and specialists relates to ecological tolerance, competitive ability, and vulnerability to environmental change. Connect to ecological tolerance, adaptations, biodiversity, endangered species, and K-selected vs. r-selected species. AP exam note: students must classify species and predict their vulnerability to habitat change. Vocabulary: generalist, specialist, ecological niche, fundamental niche, realized niche, habitat, resource partitioning, adaptability, extinction risk.

Populations.skill: ERT-3.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Populations - 3.2 K-Selected and r-Selected Species
Populations.prompt: This topic covers K-selected and r-selected species for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-3.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that r-selected species emphasize high reproductive rates with many offspring, little parental care, early maturity, and short lifespans (e.g., insects, bacteria, rodents). They thrive in unstable environments and are often pioneer species in succession. K-selected species emphasize survival and competitive ability with few offspring, extensive parental care, late maturity, and long lifespans (e.g., elephants, whales, humans). They thrive in stable environments near carrying capacity. Most species fall along a continuum between these extremes. K-selected species are generally more vulnerable to extinction due to slow reproduction rates and smaller population sizes. Connect to survivorship curves, carrying capacity, population growth, ecological succession, and conservation. AP exam note: students must compare life history traits and predict how each type responds to environmental disturbance. Vocabulary: r-selected, K-selected, carrying capacity, reproductive rate, life history strategy, parental care, pioneer species, opportunistic species.

This topic covers survivorship curves for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that survivorship curves are graphs showing the proportion of individuals surviving at each age in a population. There are three idealized types: Type I curves show high survival rates throughout most of life with high mortality in old age (e.g., humans, elephants, whales); these are typical of K-selected species. Type II curves show a constant mortality rate throughout life (e.g., songbirds, some lizards, rodents). Type III curves show very high mortality early in life with high survival for the few that reach adulthood (e.g., fish, sea turtles, oysters, insects); these are typical of r-selected species. The shape of the curve reflects a species' life history strategy and reproductive investment. Connect to K-selected and r-selected species, population dynamics, and carrying capacity. AP exam note: students must interpret survivorship curves and relate them to reproductive strategies. Vocabulary: survivorship curve, Type I, Type II, Type III, mortality rate, life expectancy, life history, cohort.

This topic covers carrying capacity for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely given the available resources, including food, water, shelter, and space. When a population is below K, it can grow; as it approaches K, growth slows due to increasing competition for limited resources (density-dependent factors). Populations may temporarily exceed K (overshoot), leading to a die-off or crash. Carrying capacity is not static; it can change with environmental conditions, resource availability, and human activities. Density-dependent limiting factors (competition, predation, disease, parasitism) intensify as population density increases. Density-independent factors (natural disasters, climate extremes) affect populations regardless of size. Connect to logistic growth, resource availability, and human population dynamics. AP exam note: students must interpret population graphs showing carrying capacity and explain factors that alter K. Vocabulary: carrying capacity, density-dependent, density-independent, limiting factor, overshoot, die-off, logistic growth, resources.

Populations.skill: EIN-1.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) Populations - 3.5 Population Growth and Resource Availability
Populations.prompt: This topic covers population growth and resource availability for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand two models of population growth: exponential growth and logistic growth. Exponential growth (J-shaped curve) occurs when resources are unlimited and the population grows at a constant rate (dN/dt = rN), where r is the intrinsic rate of increase and N is population size. Logistic growth (S-shaped curve) incorporates carrying capacity (K) and is modeled by dN/dt = rN((K-N)/K), where growth slows as N approaches K. The maximum growth rate occurs at N = K/2. Resource availability is the primary factor determining whether a population grows exponentially or logistically. Students must calculate growth rates, doubling time (Rule of 70: doubling time = 70/growth rate%), and interpret population growth graphs. Connect to carrying capacity, human population dynamics, and sustainability. AP exam note: quantitative calculations involving growth rate and carrying capacity are frequently tested. Vocabulary: exponential growth, logistic growth, intrinsic rate of increase, doubling time, Rule of 70, J-curve, S-curve.

Populations.skill: EIN-1.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) Populations - 3.6 Age Structure Diagrams
Populations.prompt: This topic covers age structure diagrams for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that age structure diagrams (population pyramids) are bar graphs showing the distribution of a population across age groups and sex. They are divided into pre-reproductive, reproductive, and post-reproductive cohorts. A pyramid with a wide base (many young individuals) indicates rapid growth, typical of developing countries. A column-shaped diagram (relatively equal bars) indicates slow or zero growth, typical of developed countries. An inverted pyramid (narrow base, wide top) indicates population decline. Age structure diagrams can predict future population trends, resource needs, and social challenges such as dependency ratios. Countries with rapidly growing populations face challenges in education, employment, and resource management; aging populations face workforce shortages and healthcare demands. Connect to demographic transition, total fertility rate, and human population dynamics. AP exam note: students must interpret and compare age structure diagrams from different countries. Vocabulary: age structure diagram, population pyramid, pre-reproductive, reproductive, post-reproductive, dependency ratio, growth rate.

Populations.skill: EIN-1.D (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) Populations - 3.7 Total Fertility Rate
Populations.prompt: This topic covers total fertility rate for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that total fertility rate (TFR) is the average number of children a woman is expected to have during her childbearing years (typically ages 15-49). Replacement-level fertility is approximately 2.1 children per woman in developed countries, accounting for infant and child mortality. A TFR above 2.1 leads to population growth; below 2.1, population eventually declines (though momentum may delay this). TFR varies significantly between countries and is influenced by factors including access to education (especially for women), healthcare, contraception, cultural norms, economic development, government policies, and urbanization. Developing nations tend to have higher TFR, while industrialized nations have lower TFR. Connect to age structure diagrams, demographic transition, and human population dynamics. AP exam note: students must analyze TFR data and explain the social, economic, and environmental factors that influence fertility rates. Vocabulary: total fertility rate, replacement-level fertility, infant mortality rate, contraception, population momentum, birth rate, demographic data.

Populations.skill: EIN-1.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.8) Populations - 3.8 Human Population Dynamics
Populations.prompt: This topic covers human population dynamics for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand that the global human population has grown exponentially, particularly since the Industrial Revolution, due to advances in agriculture, medicine, sanitation, and technology. Current population exceeds 8 billion. Population growth rate is calculated as: (birth rate + immigration) - (death rate + emigration). Doubling time is estimated using the Rule of 70. Population growth varies dramatically by region: Sub-Saharan Africa has the highest growth rates, while Europe and Japan have declining populations. Population momentum means that even after TFR drops to replacement level, populations continue to grow because of the large number of individuals in reproductive age groups. Rapid population growth intensifies environmental pressures including resource depletion, habitat destruction, and pollution. Connect to carrying capacity, ecological footprint, sustainability, and demographic transition. AP exam note: students must perform growth rate and doubling time calculations and analyze population trends. Vocabulary: population growth rate, doubling time, Rule of 70, population momentum, crude birth rate, crude death rate, immigration, emigration.

This topic covers demographic transition for AP Environmental Science students (CED: EIN-1.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that the demographic transition model (DTM) describes how population growth changes as countries industrialize and develop economically. It consists of four stages: Stage 1 (pre-industrial) features high birth and death rates, resulting in slow population growth. Stage 2 (transitional) features high birth rates but declining death rates due to improved healthcare, sanitation, and food supply, causing rapid population growth. Stage 3 (industrial) features declining birth rates due to urbanization, education, and access to contraception, slowing growth. Stage 4 (post-industrial) features low birth and death rates, resulting in stable or declining population. Some models include Stage 5, where birth rates fall below death rates, leading to population decline. The demographic transition correlates with economic development, education levels, and women's empowerment. Connect to age structure diagrams, TFR, and human population dynamics. AP exam note: students must identify stages and predict population trends from demographic data. Vocabulary: demographic transition model, industrialization, birth rate, death rate, urbanization, economic development, population stabilization.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.1 Plate Tectonics
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers plate tectonics for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that Earth's lithosphere is divided into tectonic plates that float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere and move due to convection currents in the mantle. Three types of plate boundaries exist: divergent (plates move apart, creating mid-ocean ridges and rift valleys), convergent (plates collide, forming mountains, trenches, and subduction zones), and transform (plates slide past each other, causing earthquakes). Volcanic eruptions and earthquakes are concentrated along plate boundaries (Ring of Fire). Seafloor spreading at divergent boundaries creates new oceanic crust, while subduction at convergent boundaries recycles crust. Plate tectonics drives the rock cycle, shapes Earth's surface, creates mineral deposits, and influences species distribution through continental drift. Connect to soil formation, natural hazards, resource distribution, and climate patterns. AP exam note: students must identify boundary types and explain associated geological events. Vocabulary: tectonic plates, divergent, convergent, transform, subduction, mid-ocean ridge, Ring of Fire, convection, lithosphere, asthenosphere.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.2 Soil Formation and Erosion
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers soil formation and erosion for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that soil forms through the weathering of parent rock (physical, chemical, and biological weathering) combined with the addition of organic matter over hundreds to thousands of years. Soil develops in layers called horizons: O horizon (organic matter/humus), A horizon (topsoil, mix of organic matter and minerals), B horizon (subsoil, accumulated minerals leached from above), C horizon (weathered parent rock), and R horizon (bedrock). Soil erosion occurs when wind, water, or human activities remove topsoil faster than it can form. Major causes of accelerated erosion include deforestation, overgrazing, conventional tillage, and construction. Erosion reduces soil fertility, degrades water quality through sedimentation, and threatens food security. Prevention strategies include contour plowing, terracing, cover crops, no-till farming, and windbreaks. Connect to agriculture, water quality, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must identify soil horizons and explain erosion causes and prevention methods. Vocabulary: soil horizon, topsoil, humus, weathering, erosion, sedimentation, contour plowing, terracing, no-till farming.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.C (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.3 Soil Composition and Properties
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers soil composition and properties for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that soil is composed of approximately 45% mineral particles, 25% water, 25% air, and 5% organic matter. Mineral particles are classified by size: sand (largest, 0.05-2 mm), silt (medium, 0.002-0.05 mm), and clay (smallest, <0.002 mm). The soil texture triangle is used to classify soil types based on the proportions of sand, silt, and clay. Loam, a balanced mixture, is generally ideal for agriculture. Sandy soils drain quickly and hold fewer nutrients; clay soils retain water and nutrients but have poor drainage and aeration. Soil pH affects nutrient availability: most crops prefer slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0-7.0). Cation exchange capacity (CEC) measures the soil's ability to hold positively charged nutrients. Organic matter (humus) improves soil structure, water retention, and nutrient availability. Connect to agriculture, erosion, the phosphorus cycle, and land management. AP exam note: students must interpret soil texture triangles and relate soil properties to agricultural productivity. Vocabulary: soil texture, sand, silt, clay, loam, soil texture triangle, porosity, permeability, pH, cation exchange capacity, humus.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.4) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.4 Earth's Atmosphere
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers Earth's atmosphere for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that Earth's atmosphere is divided into layers based on temperature changes with altitude: the troposphere (0-12 km, where weather occurs and temperature decreases with altitude), stratosphere (12-50 km, contains the ozone layer and temperature increases with altitude), mesosphere (50-80 km, temperature decreases), and thermosphere (80-700 km, temperature increases due to absorption of solar radiation). The atmosphere is composed of approximately 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 0.9% argon, and 0.04% carbon dioxide, with trace amounts of other gases and variable water vapor. The troposphere contains approximately 75% of atmospheric mass and virtually all weather phenomena. The ozone layer in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV radiation, protecting life on Earth. Connect to the greenhouse effect, air pollution, ozone depletion, climate, and wind patterns. AP exam note: students must identify atmospheric layers and their characteristics and explain the role of the ozone layer. Vocabulary: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere, ozone layer, atmospheric composition, UV radiation, weather, lapse rate.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.E (Unit 4, Topic 4.5) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.5 Global Wind Patterns
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers global wind patterns for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Students should understand that global wind patterns are driven by uneven solar heating of Earth's surface, the Coriolis effect (deflection due to Earth's rotation), and convection cells. Three major convection cells exist in each hemisphere: Hadley cells (0-30 degrees latitude), Ferrel cells (30-60 degrees), and Polar cells (60-90 degrees). These create predictable surface wind patterns: trade winds (easterlies between 0-30 degrees), westerlies (30-60 degrees), and polar easterlies (60-90 degrees). The Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, creating a belt of rising air and heavy precipitation. Upwelling zones occur along coasts where wind pushes surface water away, bringing nutrient-rich deep water to the surface. Connect to biome distribution, ocean currents, weather, and climate. AP exam note: students must explain how convection cells and the Coriolis effect create global wind patterns. Vocabulary: convection cell, Hadley cell, Ferrel cell, Polar cell, Coriolis effect, trade winds, westerlies, ITCZ, upwelling.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.F (Unit 4, Topic 4.6) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.6 Watersheds
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers watersheds for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Students should understand that a watershed (drainage basin) is an area of land where all precipitation drains into a common body of water such as a river, lake, or ocean. Watersheds are separated by ridgelines called divides. Every point on land is part of a watershed, and they range in size from small creeks to massive river systems like the Mississippi River watershed. Healthy watersheds provide critical ecosystem services including water filtration, flood control, groundwater recharge, and habitat for aquatic and terrestrial species. Human activities within a watershed directly affect water quality downstream: urbanization increases impervious surfaces and runoff, agriculture adds sediment and chemicals, and deforestation accelerates erosion. Nonpoint source pollution from multiple locations across a watershed is the leading cause of water quality degradation. Connect to the water cycle, water pollution, urbanization, and land use management. AP exam note: students must trace pollutant pathways through a watershed and propose management strategies. Vocabulary: watershed, drainage basin, divide, impervious surface, runoff, nonpoint source pollution, point source pollution, groundwater recharge, riparian zone.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.G (Unit 4, Topic 4.7) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.7 Solar Radiation and Earth's Seasons
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers solar radiation and Earth's seasons for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.G, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). Students should understand that Earth's seasons are caused by the 23.5-degree tilt of Earth's axis relative to its orbital plane, not by distance from the Sun. This tilt causes variations in the angle of incoming solar radiation (insolation) and day length throughout the year. When the Northern Hemisphere is tilted toward the Sun, it experiences summer (more direct sunlight, longer days), while the Southern Hemisphere experiences winter. The equator receives relatively consistent solar radiation year-round, while the poles experience extreme seasonal variation. Albedo (reflectivity) affects how much solar radiation is absorbed versus reflected; ice, snow, and light surfaces have high albedo, while dark surfaces (oceans, forests) have low albedo. Differential heating of Earth's surface drives wind patterns, ocean currents, and weather systems. Connect to climate, biome distribution, global wind patterns, and the greenhouse effect. AP exam note: students must explain how axial tilt causes seasonal changes in insolation. Vocabulary: insolation, axial tilt, albedo, solstice, equinox, latitude, direct sunlight, angle of incidence.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.H (Unit 4, Topic 4.8) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.8 Earth's Geography and Climate
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers Earth's geography and climate for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.H, Unit 4, Topic 4.8). Students should understand that climate is the long-term average of weather patterns in a region, influenced by latitude, altitude, proximity to large bodies of water, ocean currents, topography, and prevailing winds. Latitude is the primary determinant of climate zones (tropical, temperate, polar) because it controls insolation. Altitude affects climate similarly to latitude: temperature decreases approximately 6.5 degrees Celsius per 1,000 meters of elevation gain. The rain shadow effect occurs when moist air rises over a mountain range, cools, and precipitates on the windward side, leaving the leeward side dry. Ocean currents (such as the Gulf Stream) moderate coastal temperatures by redistributing heat. Urban heat islands occur where cities are warmer than surrounding rural areas due to impervious surfaces, waste heat, and reduced vegetation. Connect to biomes, wind patterns, weather events, and climate change. AP exam note: students must explain how geographic factors determine regional climates. Vocabulary: climate, weather, latitude, altitude, rain shadow, ocean current, urban heat island, windward, leeward, microclimate.

Earth Systems and Resources.skill: ERT-4.I (Unit 4, Topic 4.9) Earth Systems and Resources - 4.9 El Niño and La Niña
Earth Systems and Resources.prompt: This topic covers El Niño and La Niña for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ERT-4.I, Unit 4, Topic 4.9). Students should understand that El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a periodic climate pattern involving changes in sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. During El Niño, trade winds weaken or reverse, warm water accumulates in the eastern Pacific, and upwelling decreases along South America. This causes increased rainfall and flooding in the eastern Pacific (Peru, Ecuador) and drought in the western Pacific (Australia, Southeast Asia). During La Niña, trade winds strengthen, upwelling intensifies, and conditions are opposite: the western Pacific gets more rain while the eastern Pacific is drier. ENSO events typically occur every 2-7 years and last 9-12 months. Global impacts include disruptions to fisheries, agriculture, and weather patterns far from the Pacific. Connect to ocean currents, global wind patterns, climate, and biodiversity. AP exam note: students must compare El Niño and La Niña conditions and describe their global environmental effects. Vocabulary: El Niño, La Niña, ENSO, trade winds, upwelling, sea surface temperature, thermocline, Southern Oscillation, teleconnection.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.1) Land and Water Use - 5.1 The Tragedy of the Commons
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers the tragedy of the commons for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that the tragedy of the commons, described by Garrett Hardin in 1968, occurs when individuals acting in their own self-interest overexploit shared resources, degrading them for everyone. Examples include overfishing in international waters, overgrazing on public lands, air and water pollution, deforestation of shared forests, and depletion of groundwater aquifers. The concept illustrates the conflict between individual benefit and collective well-being. Solutions include government regulation (quotas, permits, protected areas), privatization of resources, community-based management, and international treaties. Cap-and-trade programs and tradable permits are market-based approaches that address the tragedy of the commons for pollution. Connect to sustainability, resource management, fisheries, public lands, and international environmental agreements. AP exam note: students must identify real-world examples and propose solutions to commons dilemmas. Vocabulary: tragedy of the commons, common-pool resources, overexploitation, externality, regulation, privatization, cap-and-trade, quota, sustainability.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Land and Water Use - 5.2 Clearcutting
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers clearcutting for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that clearcutting is a logging practice in which all trees in an area are harvested at once. While economically efficient and useful for shade-intolerant species that regenerate best in full sunlight, clearcutting has significant environmental impacts: soil erosion and compaction, increased sedimentation in waterways, loss of habitat and biodiversity, disruption of nutrient cycling, altered microclimates, increased runoff and flooding, and aesthetic degradation. Deforestation, including clearcutting, contributes to climate change by releasing stored carbon and reducing carbon sequestration capacity. Alternative forestry practices include selective cutting (removing individual high-value trees), shelterwood cutting (gradual harvesting in stages), and strip cutting (clearcutting narrow strips to allow natural regeneration from adjacent forest). Sustainable forestry is certified by organizations like the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC). Connect to carbon cycle, soil erosion, biodiversity, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must compare logging methods and evaluate their environmental trade-offs. Vocabulary: clearcutting, selective cutting, shelterwood cutting, deforestation, erosion, sedimentation, carbon sequestration, sustainable forestry.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Land and Water Use - 5.3 The Green Revolution
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers the Green Revolution for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that the Green Revolution (1940s-1960s) was a period of agricultural transformation that dramatically increased crop yields worldwide through the development of high-yield crop varieties (HYVs), widespread use of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides, mechanization, and irrigation systems. Key figures include Norman Borlaug, who developed high-yield, disease-resistant wheat varieties. Benefits included reduced famine and increased food production, particularly in developing countries (India, Mexico, Philippines). However, the Green Revolution also had significant negative consequences: increased dependence on fossil fuels and agrochemicals, water depletion from irrigation, soil degradation, loss of crop genetic diversity (monocultures), pollution from fertilizer runoff (eutrophication), health risks from pesticide exposure, and displacement of small-scale farmers. Connect to agricultural practices, sustainability, water use, pollution, and food security. AP exam note: students must evaluate both the benefits and environmental costs of the Green Revolution. Vocabulary: Green Revolution, high-yield varieties, monoculture, mechanization, synthetic fertilizer, pesticide, food security, genetic diversity.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Land and Water Use - 5.4 Impacts of Agricultural Practices
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers impacts of agricultural practices for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that modern industrial agriculture, while productive, has widespread environmental impacts. Soil impacts include erosion from tilling, compaction from heavy machinery, salinization from irrigation, and nutrient depletion from monocultures. Water impacts include contamination from fertilizer and pesticide runoff, eutrophication of waterways, and depletion of aquifers. Air impacts include greenhouse gas emissions (CO2 from machinery, methane from livestock, N2O from fertilizers). Biodiversity impacts include habitat destruction from land conversion, loss of pollinators from pesticide use, and genetic erosion from monocultures. Slash-and-burn agriculture (swidden) converts forest to farmland but releases carbon and degrades soil. Overgrazing leads to desertification. Waterlogging and salinization reduce agricultural productivity in irrigated regions. Connect to soil formation, water pollution, climate change, and sustainable agriculture. AP exam note: students must identify specific environmental impacts and propose mitigation strategies. Vocabulary: monoculture, salinization, waterlogging, desertification, slash-and-burn, erosion, eutrophication, runoff, agrochemical, overgrazing.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.E (Unit 5, Topic 5.5) Land and Water Use - 5.5 Irrigation Methods
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers irrigation methods for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.E, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand the major irrigation methods and their environmental trade-offs. Flood (furrow) irrigation is the oldest and least efficient method (~60% efficient), channeling water across fields by gravity; it leads to waterlogging, salinization, and significant water loss through evaporation. Spray (sprinkler) irrigation (~75% efficient) uses pressurized systems to distribute water but loses water to evaporation and wind drift. Drip (micro) irrigation (~95% efficient) delivers water directly to plant roots through small tubes and emitters, minimizing waste and reducing salinization and waterlogging. Gravity-fed systems from canals or aqueducts are used widely but can deplete rivers and aquifers. The Ogallala Aquifer in the US Great Plains is a critical example of groundwater depletion from over-irrigation. Salinization occurs when irrigation water evaporates and leaves mineral salts behind, reducing soil productivity. Connect to water resources, agricultural impacts, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must compare irrigation methods by efficiency and environmental impact. Vocabulary: flood irrigation, drip irrigation, sprinkler irrigation, salinization, waterlogging, aquifer depletion, water efficiency, Ogallala Aquifer.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.F (Unit 5, Topic 5.6) Land and Water Use - 5.6 Pest Control Methods
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers pest control methods for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.F, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand the major approaches to controlling agricultural pests. Chemical pesticides (insecticides, herbicides, fungicides, rodenticides) are effective but have significant drawbacks: pesticide resistance through natural selection, bioaccumulation and biomagnification in food chains, harm to non-target species (including pollinators and beneficial insects), water contamination, and human health risks. Biological control uses natural predators, parasites, or pathogens to control pest populations (e.g., introducing ladybugs to control aphids). Genetic engineering produces pest-resistant crop varieties (e.g., Bt crops). Other methods include crop rotation, intercropping, use of pheromone traps, and physical barriers. First-generation pesticides were natural compounds; second-generation pesticides are synthetic chemicals (DDT, organophosphates). DDT was banned in the US in 1972 due to bioaccumulation and effects on bird reproduction (eggshell thinning). Connect to biomagnification, integrated pest management, and environmental health. AP exam note: students must evaluate pest control strategies and explain pesticide resistance. Vocabulary: pesticide, insecticide, herbicide, bioaccumulation, biomagnification, biological control, pesticide resistance, DDT, Bt crops.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.G (Unit 5, Topic 5.7) Land and Water Use - 5.7 Meat Production Methods
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers meat production methods for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.G, Unit 5, Topic 5.7). Students should understand that meat production has significant environmental impacts, largely due to the inefficiency of energy transfer between trophic levels (10% rule). Concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) or factory farms raise large numbers of livestock in confined spaces. Benefits include efficient production and lower costs. Environmental impacts include large volumes of manure causing water pollution, methane emissions from enteric fermentation (especially cattle), antibiotic resistance from overuse of antibiotics, deforestation for grazing land (especially in the Amazon), high water consumption, and grain diversion from human consumption to animal feed. Approximately 10 kg of grain is needed to produce 1 kg of beef. Free-range and grass-fed operations may reduce some impacts but require more land. Reducing meat consumption, especially beef, is one of the most effective individual actions to reduce environmental impact. Connect to energy flow, trophic levels, water pollution, climate change, and land use. AP exam note: students must apply the 10% rule to explain the inefficiency of meat production. Vocabulary: CAFO, concentrated animal feeding operation, enteric fermentation, methane, trophic efficiency, factory farming, free-range, antibiotic resistance.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.H (Unit 5, Topic 5.8) Land and Water Use - 5.8 Impacts of Overfishing
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers impacts of overfishing for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.H, Unit 5, Topic 5.8). Students should understand that overfishing occurs when fish are harvested faster than they can reproduce, leading to population decline and potential collapse. The tragedy of the commons applies directly to ocean fisheries, as international waters lack effective regulation. Major impacts include depletion of commercially important species (Atlantic cod, bluefin tuna), disruption of marine food webs, bycatch (unintended capture of non-target species including dolphins, sea turtles, and seabirds), habitat destruction from bottom trawling, and loss of biodiversity. The maximum sustainable yield (MSY) is the largest catch that can be taken indefinitely without depleting the population. Many fisheries have exceeded MSY, resulting in collapse. Solutions include fishing quotas, marine protected areas, catch limits, seasonal closures, mesh size regulations, aquaculture (fish farming), and international treaties. Aquaculture has its own environmental concerns, including waste pollution, disease spread, and habitat conversion. Connect to tragedy of the commons, food webs, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must explain overfishing causes and evaluate management solutions. Vocabulary: overfishing, bycatch, maximum sustainable yield, bottom trawling, aquaculture, fish stock, marine protected area, quota.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.I (Unit 5, Topic 5.9) Land and Water Use - 5.9 Impacts of Mining
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers impacts of mining for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.I, Unit 5, Topic 5.9). Students should understand that mining extracts minerals, metals, and fossil fuels from the Earth, providing essential resources but causing significant environmental damage. Surface mining methods include strip mining (removing layers of soil and rock), open-pit mining (excavating large pits), and mountaintop removal (blasting mountaintops to access coal seams). Subsurface mining involves digging tunnels underground and is less destructive to surface ecosystems but poses risks to miners. Environmental impacts include habitat destruction and fragmentation, soil erosion and degradation, acid mine drainage (sulfuric acid from exposed sulfide minerals contaminating waterways), heavy metal contamination of soil and water, air pollution from dust and processing, groundwater contamination, and large volumes of tailings (waste rock). The Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act (1977) requires restoration of mined land in the US. Connect to resource use, water pollution, land degradation, and environmental legislation. AP exam note: students must compare mining methods and explain acid mine drainage formation. Vocabulary: strip mining, open-pit mining, mountaintop removal, subsurface mining, acid mine drainage, tailings, reclamation, ore, overburden.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.J (Unit 5, Topic 5.10) Land and Water Use - 5.10 Impacts of Urbanization
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers impacts of urbanization for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.J, Unit 5, Topic 5.10). Students should understand that urbanization is the migration of populations from rural to urban areas, with over 55% of the world's population now living in cities. Environmental impacts include habitat destruction and fragmentation from urban sprawl, increased impervious surfaces causing greater stormwater runoff and reduced groundwater recharge, urban heat island effect (cities are warmer than surrounding areas due to heat absorption by concrete and asphalt), increased air and water pollution, higher energy consumption, and waste generation. Urban sprawl, the spread of low-density development into previously undeveloped areas, leads to increased automobile dependence, loss of agricultural land, and higher per-capita resource consumption. Smart growth strategies include mixed-use development, public transit, green spaces, infill development, and urban growth boundaries. Green infrastructure (green roofs, permeable pavements, rain gardens) can mitigate runoff and heat. Connect to watersheds, air pollution, energy use, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must identify urbanization impacts and evaluate mitigation strategies. Vocabulary: urbanization, urban sprawl, impervious surface, urban heat island, smart growth, green infrastructure, infill development, stormwater runoff.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-1.K (Unit 5, Topic 5.11) Land and Water Use - 5.11 Ecological Footprints
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers ecological footprints for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-1.K, Unit 5, Topic 5.11). Students should understand that an ecological footprint measures the amount of biologically productive land and water required to support a person's resource consumption and absorb their waste, expressed in global hectares. It includes the land needed for food production, housing, transportation, goods, services, and carbon absorption. If humanity's total ecological footprint exceeds Earth's biocapacity (ecological overshoot), resources are being consumed unsustainably. Currently, humanity uses approximately 1.7 Earths' worth of resources annually. Ecological footprints vary dramatically between nations: high-income countries (US, Australia, Europe) have much larger footprints than low-income countries. Per capita footprints are influenced by diet (meat-heavy diets have larger footprints), transportation patterns, housing, and consumption levels. Reducing ecological footprints involves consuming less, increasing efficiency, and shifting to renewable resources. Connect to sustainability, resource use, carrying capacity, and population dynamics. AP exam note: students must compare ecological footprints across nations and propose strategies to reduce them. Vocabulary: ecological footprint, biocapacity, ecological overshoot, global hectare, per capita consumption, sustainability, resource consumption, carbon footprint.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-2.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.12) Land and Water Use - 5.12 Introduction to Sustainability
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers introduction to sustainability for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-2.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.12). Students should understand that sustainability means meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs (Brundtland Commission, 1987). Sustainable development integrates three pillars: environmental (protecting ecosystems and natural resources), economic (maintaining viable economies), and social (ensuring equity and quality of life). Key sustainability practices include using renewable resources within their replenishment rate, minimizing waste through reduce-reuse-recycle, maintaining biodiversity, reducing carbon emissions, and implementing sustainable agriculture and forestry. The concept of natural capital recognizes that ecosystem services have economic value. Environmental indicators like ecological footprint, carbon footprint, and the Environmental Performance Index help measure progress toward sustainability. Connect to resource management, ecological footprints, renewable energy, and conservation. AP exam note: students must define sustainability and apply it to evaluate real-world resource management scenarios. Vocabulary: sustainability, sustainable development, natural capital, renewable resource, nonrenewable resource, conservation, preservation, stewardship, Brundtland Commission.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-2.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.13) Land and Water Use - 5.13 Methods to Reduce Urban Runoff
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers methods to reduce urban runoff for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-2.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.13). Students should understand that urban runoff is a major source of water pollution, carrying sediment, oil, heavy metals, pesticides, fertilizers, bacteria, and thermal pollution into waterways. Impervious surfaces (roads, parking lots, rooftops) prevent infiltration and increase runoff volume and velocity. Methods to reduce urban runoff include: permeable pavement that allows water to infiltrate, green roofs (vegetated rooftops) that absorb rainwater, rain gardens and bioswales that capture and filter runoff, retention and detention ponds that collect stormwater, constructed wetlands that naturally filter pollutants, rain barrels and cisterns that collect roof runoff for reuse, riparian buffers (vegetated strips along waterways) that filter runoff, and reducing impervious surfaces through smart growth planning. Low Impact Development (LID) mimics natural hydrology to manage stormwater at its source. Connect to watersheds, urbanization, water pollution, and green infrastructure. AP exam note: students must explain how specific methods reduce runoff and improve water quality. Vocabulary: urban runoff, impervious surface, permeable pavement, green roof, rain garden, bioswale, retention pond, riparian buffer, Low Impact Development, infiltration.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-2.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.14) Land and Water Use - 5.14 Integrated Pest Management
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers integrated pest management for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-2.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.14). Students should understand that integrated pest management (IPM) is an ecologically-based approach that combines multiple strategies to control pests effectively while minimizing environmental and health risks. IPM uses a tiered approach: first, prevention through cultural practices (crop rotation, resistant varieties, planting timing, intercropping); second, monitoring to assess pest levels and determine if action thresholds are exceeded; third, biological control (introducing natural predators, parasitoids, or pathogens); fourth, mechanical control (traps, barriers, hand removal); and finally, targeted chemical control as a last resort, using the least toxic pesticide at the appropriate time and rate. IPM aims to keep pest populations below economically damaging levels rather than eliminate them entirely. Benefits include reduced pesticide use, lower costs over time, decreased environmental contamination, and slower development of pesticide resistance. Connect to pest control methods, sustainable agriculture, and biodiversity. AP exam note: students must explain IPM principles and contrast IPM with conventional pesticide-based approaches. Vocabulary: integrated pest management, action threshold, biological control, cultural control, mechanical control, pest monitoring, economic injury level, natural enemy.

Land and Water Use.skill: STB-2.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.15) Land and Water Use - 5.15 Sustainable Agriculture
Land and Water Use.prompt: This topic covers sustainable agriculture for AP Environmental Science students (CED: STB-2.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.15). Students should understand that sustainable agriculture aims to produce food while protecting the environment, maintaining economic viability, and supporting communities for the long term. Key practices include crop rotation (alternating crops to maintain soil fertility and reduce pest buildup), cover cropping (planting non-cash crops to prevent erosion and add nutrients), no-till or reduced-till farming (minimizing soil disturbance to reduce erosion and maintain soil structure), contour plowing (plowing along hillside contours to reduce water runoff), terracing (creating flat platforms on slopes), intercropping and polyculture (growing multiple crops together to increase biodiversity), agroforestry (integrating trees with crops or livestock), composting (recycling organic waste into soil amendments), and drip irrigation (efficient water delivery). Organic farming avoids synthetic pesticides and fertilizers. Locally grown and seasonal food reduces transportation emissions. Connect to soil conservation, water quality, IPM, and food security. AP exam note: students must identify and explain multiple sustainable practices and their environmental benefits. Vocabulary: sustainable agriculture, crop rotation, cover crop, no-till farming, contour plowing, terracing, polyculture, agroforestry, organic farming, composting.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.1 Renewable and Nonrenewable Resources
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers renewable and nonrenewable resources for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand the distinction between renewable and nonrenewable resources. Renewable resources are replenished naturally on a human timescale and include solar energy, wind, hydropower, geothermal energy, and biomass. Nonrenewable resources exist in finite quantities and are consumed faster than they form; these include fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) and nuclear fuel (uranium). Some resources are considered potentially renewable if managed sustainably (soil, freshwater, forests) but can become depleted if overused. The availability and use of energy resources depend on economic, technological, political, and environmental factors. Currently, approximately 80% of global energy comes from fossil fuels. The transition to renewable energy is essential for sustainability but faces challenges including intermittency, storage, infrastructure, and cost. Connect to energy consumption, climate change, and sustainability. AP exam note: students must classify resources and evaluate trade-offs between renewable and nonrenewable energy. Vocabulary: renewable resource, nonrenewable resource, fossil fuel, solar energy, wind energy, hydropower, geothermal, biomass, sustainability, intermittency.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.2 Global Energy Consumption
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers global energy consumption for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that global energy consumption has increased dramatically since the Industrial Revolution and continues to rise with population growth and economic development. Energy use varies significantly between developed and developing nations: developed countries consume far more energy per capita but developing countries are increasing consumption rapidly, especially China and India. The majority of global energy still comes from fossil fuels: oil (~33%), coal (~27%), and natural gas (~24%), with the remainder from hydropower, nuclear, and other renewables. Energy use patterns are influenced by industrialization level, population, climate, transportation infrastructure, and government policies. Energy intensity (energy consumed per unit of GDP) has been declining in many countries due to efficiency improvements, but total consumption continues to rise. The environmental consequences of high energy consumption include climate change, air and water pollution, and habitat destruction. Connect to fossil fuels, renewable energy, ecological footprint, and climate change. AP exam note: students must analyze energy consumption data and trends across countries. Vocabulary: energy consumption, per capita energy use, energy intensity, fossil fuels, primary energy, developed nation, developing nation, energy mix.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.3 Fuel Types and Uses
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers fuel types and uses for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand the major fuel types and their applications. Coal is primarily used for electricity generation and industrial processes; it has the highest carbon emissions per unit of energy. Petroleum (oil) is primarily used for transportation and is the basis of plastics and chemicals. Natural gas is used for electricity generation, heating, and cooking; it produces fewer CO2 emissions than coal or oil per unit of energy. Uranium is used in nuclear power plants for electricity generation. Biomass (wood, crop residue, bioethanol, biodiesel) is used for heating, cooking, and transportation fuels. The energy content of fuels varies: nuclear fuel has the highest energy density, followed by oil, natural gas, coal, and biomass. Fuel choice depends on availability, cost, infrastructure, and environmental considerations. Energy conversion efficiency varies by technology: coal plants (~33-40%), natural gas combined cycle (~60%), and electric vehicles (~85-90%). Connect to fossil fuels, renewable energy, air pollution, and climate change. AP exam note: students must compare fuel types by emissions, energy density, and applications. Vocabulary: coal, petroleum, natural gas, uranium, biomass, energy density, carbon emissions, electricity generation, combustion, energy conversion efficiency.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.D (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.4 Distribution of Natural Energy Resources
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers distribution of natural energy resources for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that energy resources are unevenly distributed across the globe, which has major geopolitical, economic, and environmental implications. Major oil reserves are concentrated in the Middle East, Venezuela, Canada, Russia, and the US. Coal reserves are largest in the US, Russia, China, India, and Australia. Natural gas reserves are concentrated in Russia, Iran, Qatar, and the US. Uranium deposits are found in Kazakhstan, Canada, Australia, and Namibia. Renewable energy potential also varies: solar resources are greatest in equatorial and desert regions, wind resources in coastal and plains areas, geothermal near tectonic boundaries, and hydropower where precipitation and topography align. Countries with abundant fossil fuels may have less incentive to transition to renewables, while resource-poor nations may depend on imports, creating energy insecurity. Connect to global energy consumption, geopolitics, fossil fuels, and renewable energy. AP exam note: students must explain how resource distribution affects energy policy and environmental outcomes. Vocabulary: energy resource distribution, reserves, geopolitics, energy security, fossil fuel dependency, OPEC, energy imports, resource curse.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.E (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.5 Fossil Fuels
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers fossil fuels for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas) formed from the remains of ancient organisms over millions of years under heat and pressure. Coal forms from ancient plant material in swamps (peat, lignite, bituminous, anthracite stages). Petroleum and natural gas form from marine organisms buried in sedimentary rock. Extraction methods include surface and subsurface mining for coal, conventional drilling and hydraulic fracturing (fracking) for oil and gas, and offshore drilling. Environmental impacts include CO2 and other greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change, air pollution (SO2, NOx, particulates), water pollution from acid mine drainage and oil spills, habitat destruction, land degradation, and groundwater contamination from fracking. Fossil fuels are nonrenewable and finite; peak oil theory suggests production will eventually decline. The combustion of fossil fuels is the primary source of anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions. Connect to climate change, air pollution, energy consumption, and renewable alternatives. AP exam note: students must compare fossil fuel types and explain extraction impacts. Vocabulary: fossil fuel, coal, petroleum, natural gas, fracking, combustion, greenhouse gas, peak oil, extraction, refining, carbon dioxide.

Energy Resources and Consumption.skill: ENG-2.F (Unit 6, Topic 6.6) Energy Resources and Consumption - 6.6 Nuclear Power
Energy Resources and Consumption.prompt: This topic covers nuclear power for AP Environmental Science students (CED: ENG-2.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that nuclear power generates electricity through nuclear fission, where uranium-235 atoms are split in a controlled chain reaction, releasing enormous amounts of heat to produce steam that drives turbines.
